title: 2025 10 09 Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-10-09 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Chained Dog Awareness NZ Trust: Mandate the Desexing of Dogs in NZ Except to Registered Breeders
topic: Petition of Chained Dog Awareness NZ Trust: Mandate the Desexing of Dogs in NZ Except to Registered Breeders
description: Petition of Chained Dog Awareness NZ Trust: Mandate the Desexing of Dogs in NZ Except to Registered Breeders Department of Internal Affairs Kate Barnes, Senior Policy Analyst Karam Talwar (Policy Analyst) Auckland Council Robert Irvine, General Manager, Licensing & Compliance Elly Waitoa, Manager, Animal Management Petition of Joshua Riley on behalf of Sovereign.nz: Allow scrutineers to watch early ballot processing in local government elections Department of Internal Affairs Amanda Shaw, Principal Policy Analyst Rowan Burns, Policy Manager
descriptionSummary: Petition of Chained Dog Awareness NZ Trust: Mandate the Desexing of Dogs in NZ Except to Registered Breeders. Department of Internal Affairs. Kate Barnes, Senior Policy Analyst

chunks.text: Today. Um, so we are here as part of our role supporting the Minister of Local Government's Oversight, the Dog Control Act. Um, so I'll make some brief comments on the dog population and the matters rights by the petition, and happy to take questions after. Um, the regulatory framework for dogs in New Zealand is, is relatively fragmented.

Um, we have the Department of Internal Affairs and the Dog Control Act. You have NPI in the animal welfare space. And then the day-to-day application and enforcement of dog control sits with the councils. Um, and we, we certainly acknowledge and recognize that, uh, there's been an increase in the dog population, um, particularly, uh, problematic dogs, roaming dogs, and that councils are facing a number of challenges responding to this.

And they work very hard every day, um, trying to, you know, keep communities safe. Um, and look, the issue of mandating desing with acknowledge that desex more dogs would likely be quite helpful in managing the dog population. Um, but there are several challenges with mandating desex.

So I'll just touch on a couple. Um, if you're gonna mandate desex and you're likely to need, uh, an exemption criteria and, and determining what that might be. And then a system for recording and registering breeders or people with an exemption. Uh, dogs are private property and for many Kiwis, they're beloved family members.

Uh, well for many dog owners, D six Center is the right choice. There are a range of reasons for may not wish to new to their dogs. Um, I'll note the New Zealand Veterinary Association submission to you outlined a number of medical factors that might be considered, uh, introducing further regulation of dog ownership. Um, specifically breeding would also have some implications as to who may be able to access or afford dog ownership.

Uh, the cost to desex might be a barrier for many households. Um, and then the resulting fines with penalties may also cause a number of issues and might not necessarily assist with compliance. Uh, breeder licensing in particular would likely require monitoring and oversight, which, um, would go beyond council's core responsibility, dog control, and would likely require welfare lens to be applied.

Um, so you'd need to consider an appropriate agency and resourcing to, uh, carry out that role. Uh, secondly, uh, there would be challenges around enforcement. As I noted, dog controls already a really challenging space working, uh, almost half of New Zealand's registered dog population is entire.

There's also a significant unregistered dog population. And, uh, it is likely that this, uh, group population has, uh, even lower rates of desex. Um, there is a potential for significant resource demand on councils if they were required to enforce desex. Um, so you can imagine, uh, would, would that look like an active and proactive enforcement?

And, and how would councils be able to resource that when already dog controllers are pretty strict space? Um, it's also not clear if the veterinary workforce would be in a position to support a significant increase in demand. Um, finally the department agrees that the act, the Dog Control Act, is increasingly not fit for purpose and addressing the needs of our dog population.

Councils and communities require a comprehensive review. At this time, the government has no plans to amend the act. However, in the meantime, our department is working on some non-regulatory improvements in partnership with the sector. And this will also include information gathering that could assist reform in the future. Um, I think that some of the previous submissions have talked about a lot of different numbers, and it's really clear that this conversation would benefit from, um, some more data, um, so that we really have a,

an accurate picture of what we are looking at. Yeah, so I'll leave it there and happy to take any questions. Yeah. Alright. Questions members? Um, members I missed, Joe. Um, as you may have seen in our submission, uh, the DA's role in the local electoral system is, is, um, is that we are responsible for the regulatory stewardship of the legislation. Uh, and as part of that, we maintain relationships with, um, the various parties that are involved in the delivery of the elections.

But it's the councils themselves that deliver the elections. Uh, they also appoint a, uh, independent electoral officer to run the elections and they also contract election service provider companies, um, to deliver the, the, uh, those, and it's those companies that do amongst other things the vote processing and counting.

Um, so this means that we'll do our best today to answer your questions if you have them. Um, but if they stray into the operational type space, then we might have to go away and talk to other people to get more information. Mm-hmm. Um, so I guess the key points from our submission, just to summarize it at the beginning, would be to say that we think it would be worthwhile to look at the role of scrutinous in the context of local elections.

Um, we don't think it's as simple as just copying the electoral Act process for parliamentary elections across, uh, largely because of the differences in the timeframes for when vote processing and counting is done for the two types of elections. But I'll explain that a little bit more, um, and that we haven't seen evidence to suggest that there's a problem here that would require urgent detention, but as I say, we agree, it's probably some an area that's worth looking at.

Um, so, uh, if shall I carry on and summarize this mission? So, um, from, I'm moving to sort of paragraphs nine to 12 here of our written submission. The, um, under the local Electoral Act, uh, vote processing involves taking the vote out of the envelope, uh, checking its validity and recording it, recording what the vote is ready for counting for postal voting.

This process can be done up to 32 and a half days before the close of polling, um, and also after the close of polling. Uh, and then moving on to paragraph 13 of our submission, scrutinies can be present for processing that takes place after the close of polling.

Um, a justice of the piece must always be present when any vote processing is done and the justice of the piece provides a certificate to say that they are satisfied that the regulatory requirements were complied with. So those are the key components of the legislation itself. Um, in our submission from paragraphs 18 to 24, we've given you a general overview of how the vote processing is done.

Um, and we've done this using information provided by the two companies. So essentially votes are processed, encountered in two processing processing centers, one in Auckland and one in Christchurch. The envelopes are opened, the papers are scanned to mark off the role and check for any irregularities in how the paper has been filled out.

The scan votes are not counted or totaled until after the close of polling. Uh, and when that happens, it's just an automated process and it occurs very quickly. So this means that there's very limited opportunity for scrutinies to observe the vote, uh, processing and counting because the process is highly automated.

Much of the processing is done before the scrutinies are allowed in the room, and the steps that are completed when they are allowed in the room are so quick. Um, and then if you compare that with parliamentary elections, the petitioner has proposed that scrutinies should be able to observe early processing similar to what occurs for parliamentary elections.

And so at paragraphs 25 to 27, we briefly summarize, uh, what the legislation, legislation says for parliamentary elections. The key difference is that no processing or counting is done for parliamentary elections until the morning of, um, election day. Um, and then both processes are done together.

They start doing the, the processing and the counting, or they can, um, with the ability for scrutinize scrutinous to observe that process. It's conducted in a restricted area with entry obviously, and exit control by the returning officer. So those factors minimize the risk that information about the voting trends can be used to influence voting.

That's still underway because it's just on that morning. Um, at paragraphs 28 to 30 of our, uh, submission, we note that, um, scrutiny provisions aren't an issue that has come up very much, uh, for the department. We had a, we had a bit of a trawl around and we found that there were a small number of comments made in select committee inquiries after local elections.

And we've outlined that in our submission just so you have the information from previous submissions. Um, but it's not an area that the department has looked into or done any real policy analysis on. Um, lastly, from paragraph 31 onwards, in our submission, we've highlighted some of the wider considerations that we think are important when thinking about further work on this issue.

Uh, the first is that obviously independent scrutiny of some sort of elections is very important. There are good reasons for that. The second is that the current provisions seem to aim to balance independent oversight by using the, having the JP there, um, with the integrity of the election.

So minimizing the risk that, um, information about votes already cast is used to influence voting. That's still underway. And, um, our last, the last thing we think is that, um, advances in technology have reduced the ability for scrutineers to observe processes to a point where it's quite, quite minimal, their ability to observe processes.

So therefore, uh, we do think it, it may be worthwhile for the role of scrutineers to be reviewed, but our suggestion is that it should be looked at as part of the end-to-end process, looking at scrutiny of the role processing, counting and recounts and thinking about that process as a whole and, and what, um, scrutiny, uh, checks and balances you have through that process.
